name: Homecare Association — Fee Rates 2025-26
description: Only 1% of contracts meet minimum price, £1.6bn funding gap
statistic: Only 1% of homecare contracts met the Homecare Association's Minimum Price benchmark for safe, regulated care in 2025-26. The average council fee rate is £24.10/hour against a calculated minimum safe price of £32.14/hour — a shortfall of £8.04 per hour. The funding gap is estimated at £1.6 billion in England alone (later revised upward to £1.9bn, with Community Care reporting £2bn short by November 2025, and a further update to £3.25bn as new employment legislation takes effect). Fee rate increases averaged just 5.6% against actual cost increases of 10-12%. 27% of local authorities are paying rates below £22.71/hour, which does not even cover statutory employment costs at the national living wage. Only 2 local authorities offered uplifts aligned with the 10% rise in provider costs. Employment costs represent 70-75% of total costs, rising to over 90% where fee rates are lowest.
date: 5 June 2025 (Fee Rates report); December 2024 (Minimum Price for Homecare 2025-26 report). Updated figures published November 2025 (£2bn shortfall) and early 2026 (£3.25bn gap with new employment law).
credibility: Sector body — the Homecare Association is the UK's membership body for homecare providers and the primary authority on homecare fee rates. Their Minimum Price for Homecare is the sector's benchmark costing model. Reports are based on FOI requests to all local authorities in England.
applicability: Directly care sector — specifically homecare/domiciliary care providers in England. The fee shortfall directly affects every state-funded homecare provider and is the sector's most-cited existential threat.
citable: Yes — published reports freely available from the Homecare Association website with full methodology. Attribute to 'Homecare Association, Fee Rates for State-Funded Homecare in 2025-26, June 2025' and 'Homecare Association, Minimum Price for Homecare 2025-26, December 2024.' Widely cited by Community Care, the sector press, and parliamentary briefings.
website_placement: Pain point section or problem-framing hero — the '1% of contracts meet minimum price' and '£1.6bn+ funding gap' are devastating headline stats that immediately establish the financial crisis context. Works powerfully as a stat block or data visualisation showing the gap between actual fee rates and the minimum safe price. Ideal for framing AI as a partial solution to the cost crisis (reducing admin overhead to improve margins at current fee levels).
emotional_vs_rational: Both — emotional trigger is very strong ('State-Sponsored Labour Exploitation' is the Homecare Association's own phrase; providers describe 'running on empty' and being unable to compete with supermarkets on pay). Rational appeal through the precise financial data: £8.04/hour shortfall, £1.6bn+ gap, 5.6% fee increase vs 10-12% cost increase. The combination makes this one of the most compelling stats in the content strategy.
clarica_differentiator: Strong differentiator. The fee shortfall means care providers cannot afford expensive software subscriptions — they need cost-effective, high-impact solutions. Clarica's agency model (bespoke AI solutions without ongoing SaaS lock-in) is better positioned than subscription-based care tech for providers operating at a loss on every hour of care. AI-driven admin efficiency can partially offset the fee gap: if AI saves 15-30% of admin time, that directly improves margins at current fee levels. Clarica can frame its services as a financial lifeline, not a luxury.
refresh_frequency: Annual — the Homecare Association publishes the Minimum Price report each December (ahead of the new financial year) and the Fee Rates survey each June (once council budgets are set). The funding gap figure has been revised upward multiple times (£1.6bn to £1.9bn to £2bn to £3.25bn). Next Minimum Price report expected December 2026; next Fee Rates report expected June 2027.
comparison_pair: Council fee rate: £24.10/hour. Minimum safe price: £32.14/hour. Gap: £8.04/hour (25% underfunding). Also: 1% of contracts meet the benchmark vs 99% that fall short. And: fee increases of 5.6% vs cost increases of 10-12% — meaning the gap widens every year without structural reform.
